text_blocks.snippet: Go is a strategy game played on a grid. Black moves first, placing one stone on an intersection. Players take turns claiming territory and capturing enemy stones. The player with the most territory and captured pieces at the end of the game wins.
snippet_highlighted_words: Black moves first, placing one stone on an intersection. Players take turns claiming territory and capturing enemy stones. The player with the most territory and captured pieces at the end of the game wins

list.snippet: Go is played on a grid of intersections. While traditional boards are 19 × 19, beginners often start on 9 × 9 or 13 × 13 boards.
Players use two colors of stones: Black and White. Black always goes first, placing the first stone on any empty intersection.
Once played, stones do not move. They are only removed if they are captured.
Liberties are the empty intersections immediately adjacent (vertically or horizontally) to a stone.
Stones of the same color connected along the lines are considered a single group. They share all the liberties of the individual stones in that group.
If all the liberties of a stone or group are blocked by the opponent, the stones are captured and removed from the board as "prisoners".
A stone or group with only one liberty remaining is in Atari (similar to "check" in chess).
You are not allowed to make a move that would exactly repeat a previous board position.

This specifically prevents the situation called Ko, where two stones endlessly capture each other back and forth. If a stone is captured in a Ko, the capturing player must play elsewhere on the board for at least one turn before their opponent can recapture it.
To keep your groups safe from being captured, they must have two "eyes". An eye is an isolated, empty space completely enclosed by your stones.
If a group has two separate eyes, the opponent can never capture it because they can only play in one eye at a time, which is not an illegal self-capture.
The game ends when both players pass consecutively (indicating there are no more profitable or safe moves to make).
A player's score is calculated by the total number of empty intersections they successfully enclosed (their territory) plus the number of opponent stones they captured.
Because Black has the advantage of going first, White is usually awarded bonus points called Komi (typically 6.5 or 7.5 points) to prevent ties.

text_blocks.snippet: For a quick, visual demonstration of the game's core concepts in action:

extracted.summary:
![How to Play Go: Setup, Strategy & Gameplay Explained](https://api.dataforseo.com/cdn/i/07210110-2120-0139-0000-7df23cf9bb0e:6)
Go is a strategy game played on a grid. `Black moves first, placing one stone on an intersection. Players take turns claiming territory and capturing enemy stones. The player with the most territory and captured pieces at the end of the game wins`.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Lh-_4fh5plI)[[3]](https://www.britgo.org/intro/intro2.html)[[4]](https://woodexpressions.com/pages/how-to-play-go-rules-setup-and-strategy)

Here are the essential rules:

1. The Board and Pieces

- Go is played on a grid of intersections. While traditional boards are 19 × 19, beginners often start on 9 × 9 or 13 × 13 boards.
- Players use two colors of stones: Black and White. Black always goes first, placing the first stone on any empty intersection.
- Once played, stones do not move . They are only removed if they are captured.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rules_of_Go)[[3]](https://www.britgo.org/intro/intro2.html)[[4]](https://woodexpressions.com/pages/how-to-play-go-rules-setup-and-strategy)

2. Liberties

- Liberties are the empty intersections immediately adjacent (vertically or horizontally) to a stone.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_%28game%29)
- Stones of the same color connected along the lines are considered a single group. They share all the liberties of the individual stones in that group.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cFVM_hwh56s)
- If all the liberties of a stone or group are blocked by the opponent, the stones are captured and removed from the board as "prisoners".[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://woodexpressions.com/pages/how-to-play-go-rules-setup-and-strategy)
- A stone or group with only one liberty remaining is in `Atari` (similar to "check" in chess).[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://woodexpressions.com/pages/how-to-play-go-rules-setup-and-strategy)

3. The Ko Rule (No Repetition)

- You are not allowed to make a move that would exactly repeat a previous board position.
- This specifically prevents the situation called `Ko` , where two stones endlessly capture each other back and forth. If a stone is captured in a Ko, the capturing player must play elsewhere on the board for at least one turn before their opponent can recapture it.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://www.mastersofgames.com/rules/go-rules.htm)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_%28game%29)

4. Eyes and Life

- To keep your groups safe from being captured, they must have two "eyes". An eye is an isolated, empty space completely enclosed by your stones.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Lh-_4fh5plI) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Lh-_4fh5plI)
- If a group has two separate eyes, the opponent can never capture it because they can only play in one eye at a time, which is not an illegal self-capture.[[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oZTdT8MQexk)

5. Winning and Scoring

- The game ends when both players pass consecutively (indicating there are no more profitable or safe moves to make).[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://woodexpressions.com/pages/how-to-play-go-rules-setup-and-strategy)
- A player's score is calculated by the total number of empty intersections they successfully enclosed (their territory) plus the number of opponent stones they captured.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)[[2]](https://woodexpressions.com/pages/how-to-play-go-rules-setup-and-strategy)
- Because Black has the advantage of going first, White is usually awarded bonus points called `Komi` (typically 6.5 or 7.5 points) to prevent ties.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VSIf7sAGSU)

For a quick, visual demonstration of the game's core concepts in action:![Related video thumbnail](https://encrypted-tbn3.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcRAMDleIh2TE2sPmdn9arS6_LziUJ0aXKmpeu8ovavlaYmw4XlM)
